## The Air-Gap Imperative for Enterprise

Enterprises in healthcare, legal, finance, government, and defense cannot send data to OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, or Mistral APIs. Reasons include:

- **HIPAA**: Protected Health Information (PHI) cannot transit external systems
- **GDPR**: Data residency requirements prohibit sending EU personal data outside approved jurisdictions
- **Attorney-Client Privilege**: Legal communications cannot be processed by third-party AI
- **Trade Secrets**: Financial models, IP, product roadmaps must not leave the network
- **Government Clearance**: Classified or sensitive data requires air-gapped processing

An air-gapped AI stack means: all inference, all embedding, all vector search, all model storage runs within your network perimeter. Not one byte of payload leaves.

## Complete Air-Gapped Stack Architecture

```
+------------------------------------------------------------------+
|                    ENTERPRISE NETWORK PERIMETER                   |
|                                                                  |
|  +-----------+    +-----------+    +-----------+    +----------+ |
|  | Document  |    | Ingestion |    | Vector DB |    | LLM      | |
|  | Sources   |--->| Pipeline  |--->| (Qdrant/  |    | Server   | |
|  | PDF, DOCX |    | (chunking |    | pgvector) |    | (Ollama/ | |
|  | emails    |    | embedding)|    |           |    | llama.cpp)| |
|  +-----------+    +-----------+    +-----------+    +----------+ |
|                         |                |               |      |
|                         v                v               v      |
|                   +----------------------------------------+    |
|                   |        RAG Orchestration Layer          |    |
|                   |   (PrivateGPT / AnythingLLM / Custom)   |    |
|                   +----------------------------------------+    |
|                                   |                             |
|                                   v                             |
|                   +----------------------------------------+    |
|                   |           User Interfaces               |    |
|                   |  Web UI / API / Slack Bot / Copilot     |    |
|                   +----------------------------------------+    |
|                                                                  |
|  NO OUTBOUND CONNECTIONS TO PUBLIC INTERNET                      |
+------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

## Component Selection for Air-Gap

| Layer | Recommended | Alternative | Avoid |
|-------|-------------|-------------|-------|
| LLM Server | Ollama | llama.cpp server | OpenAI API |
| LLM Models | Llama 3.1, Phi 4, Mistral | Qwen 2.5 | GPT-4o, Claude |
| Embedding | nomic-embed-text via Ollama | all-MiniLM via Transformers.js | OpenAI embeddings |
| Vector DB | Qdrant (self-hosted) | pgvector | Pinecone, Weaviate Cloud |
| RAG Layer | AnythingLLM (Docker) | PrivateGPT | ChatGPT Enterprise |
| Model Storage | Internal NFS/S3-compatible | Local disk | HuggingFace Hub (at runtime) |
| Model Downloads | Pre-downloaded at setup | Internal registry | Pull at runtime |

## Docker Compose Full Stack

```yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  # LLM Inference
  ollama:
    image: ollama/ollama:latest
    ports:
      - "11434:11434"
    volumes:
      - ./models:/root/.ollama  # Pre-staged models
    networks: [ai-internal]
    restart: unless-stopped

  # Vector Database
  qdrant:
    image: qdrant/qdrant:latest
    ports:
      - "6333:6333"
    volumes:
      - ./qdrant-data:/qdrant/storage
    networks: [ai-internal]
    restart: unless-stopped

  # RAG Knowledge Base
  anythingllm:
    image: mintplexlabs/anythingllm:latest
    ports:
      - "3001:3001"
    environment:
      - LLM_PROVIDER=ollama
      - OLLAMA_BASE_PATH=http://ollama:11434
      - OLLAMA_MODEL_PREF=llama3.1:8b
      - EMBEDDING_ENGINE=ollama
      - OLLAMA_EMBED_MODEL_PREF=nomic-embed-text
      - VECTOR_DB=qdrant
      - QDRANT_ENDPOINT=http://qdrant:6333
      - DISABLE_TELEMETRY=true
      - AUTH_TOKEN=${SECRET_TOKEN}
    volumes:
      - ./anythingllm-storage:/app/server/storage
    depends_on: [ollama, qdrant]
    networks: [ai-internal]
    restart: unless-stopped

networks:
  ai-internal:
    driver: bridge
    internal: true  # NO external network access
```

## Model Pre-staging for Air-Gap

```bash
# ONLINE MACHINE: Download models before deploying
ollama pull llama3.1:8b
ollama pull nomic-embed-text
ollama pull phi4

# Export models to transferable archive
tar -czf ollama-models.tar.gz ~/.ollama/models

# OFFLINE MACHINE: Restore models
mkdir -p ~/.ollama
tar -xzf ollama-models.tar.gz -C ~/

# Verify models available without internet
OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0 ollama serve &
curl http://localhost:11434/api/tags  # Should list models
```

## Network Security Controls

```bash
# UFW rules for AI server (Linux)
# Allow only internal network access to Ollama
ufw allow from 10.0.0.0/8 to any port 11434
ufw deny out to any  # Block ALL outbound
ufw allow out to 10.0.0.0/8  # Allow internal only
ufw enable

# Verify no outbound connections
ss -tunap | grep ESTABLISHED  # Should show only internal

# DNS: Configure to internal-only
echo "nameserver 10.0.0.1" > /etc/resolv.conf  # Internal DNS only
```

## Data Flow Audit Checklist

- [ ] LLM server has no outbound internet access
- [ ] Models downloaded before network isolation
- [ ] Vector DB runs on internal network only
- [ ] Document ingestion pipeline has no external calls
- [ ] Embedding model runs locally (not via external API)
- [ ] User authentication uses internal directory (LDAP/AD)
- [ ] TLS certificates from internal CA
- [ ] Logs stored internally (no external SIEM initially)
- [ ] Model updates require approval process (not auto-pull)

## Compliance Mapping

| Control | Implementation |
|---------|---------------|
| HIPAA 164.312(a)(1) - Access Control | AnythingLLM RBAC |
| HIPAA 164.312(b) - Audit Controls | AnythingLLM audit logs |
| HIPAA 164.312(e)(1) - Transmission Security | Internal TLS only |
| GDPR Art. 25 - Data Protection by Design | Local-only processing |
| GDPR Art. 32 - Security of Processing | Encryption at rest |
| SOC 2 CC6.1 - Logical Access | Role-based permissions |

## Why Local Vector Databases Matter for Privacy

A vector database stores embedding vectors for semantic similarity search. In a private AI stack, it must run locally - sending document embeddings to Pinecone or Weaviate Cloud means your document content (represented as vectors) leaves your network.

## Local Vector DB Options

### Qdrant (Recommended for Production)

```bash
# Docker
docker run -d -p 6333:6333 -p 6334:6334 \
  -v ./qdrant-storage:/qdrant/storage \
  qdrant/qdrant:latest

# Docker Compose
services:
  qdrant:
    image: qdrant/qdrant:latest
    ports: ["6333:6333"]
    volumes: ["./data:/qdrant/storage"]
    restart: unless-stopped
```

```python
from qdrant_client import QdrantClient
from qdrant_client.models import VectorParams, Distance, PointStruct

client = QdrantClient(url="http://localhost:6333")

# Create collection
client.create_collection(
    collection_name="enterprise_docs",
    vectors_config=VectorParams(
        size=768,           # Dimension of your embedding model
        distance=Distance.COSINE,
        on_disk=True,       # For large collections
    ),
)

# Insert vectors
client.upsert(
    collection_name="enterprise_docs",
    points=[
        PointStruct(
            id=1,
            vector=[0.1, 0.2, ...],  # 768-dim embedding
            payload={
                "text": "Document chunk text",
                "source": "policy-v2.3.pdf",
                "page": 4,
                "department": "HR",
            }
        )
    ]
)

# Search
results = client.search(
    collection_name="enterprise_docs",
    query_vector=query_embedding,
    limit=5,
    query_filter={
        "must": [{"key": "department", "match": {"value": "HR"}}]
    }
)
```

### ChromaDB (Simplest Local Option)

```python
import chromadb

# Persistent local storage
client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="/var/lib/chroma")

collection = client.create_collection(
    name="enterprise_docs",
    metadata={"hnsw:space": "cosine"}  # Distance metric
)

# Add documents (Chroma handles embedding if you provide a function)
collection.add(
    documents=["Document text here", "Another document"],
    metadatas=[{"source": "policy.pdf"}, {"source": "contract.pdf"}],
    ids=["doc1", "doc2"],
    embeddings=[[0.1, 0.2, ...], [0.3, 0.4, ...]]  # Pre-computed
)

# Query
results = collection.query(
    query_embeddings=[query_vector],
    n_results=5,
    where={"source": {"$contains": "policy"}}
)
```

### pgvector (Best if Already Using Postgres)

```sql
-- Enable extension
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector;

-- Create table with vector column
CREATE TABLE documents (
    id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    content TEXT NOT NULL,
    source TEXT,
    department TEXT,
    embedding vector(768),  -- Dimension matches embedding model
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Create HNSW index (fast approximate search)
CREATE INDEX ON documents
USING hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops)
WITH (m = 16, ef_construction = 64);

-- Insert document with embedding
INSERT INTO documents (content, source, embedding)
VALUES (
    'Our vacation policy allows 15 days per year...',
    'hr-policy-2025.pdf',
    '[0.1, 0.2, 0.3, ...]'::vector
);

-- Semantic search query
SELECT content, source, 1 - (embedding <=> '[...]'::vector) AS similarity
FROM documents
WHERE department = 'HR'
ORDER BY embedding <=> '[...]'::vector
LIMIT 5;
```

```python
import psycopg2
import numpy as np

conn = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://postgres@localhost:5435/postgres")
cur = conn.cursor()

# Insert with embedding
embedding = get_local_embedding("document text")
cur.execute(
    "INSERT INTO documents (content, source, embedding) VALUES (%s, %s, %s)",
    ("Document text", "source.pdf", embedding.tolist())
)

# Search
query_vec = get_local_embedding("vacation policy")
cur.execute("""
    SELECT content, source, 1 - (embedding <=> %s::vector) as score
    FROM documents
    ORDER BY embedding <=> %s::vector
    LIMIT 5
""", (query_vec.tolist(), query_vec.tolist()))

results = cur.fetchall()
```

## Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Qdrant | Chroma | pgvector |
|---------|--------|--------|----------|
| Setup complexity | Medium (Docker) | Low (pip) | Low (extension) |
| Performance | Excellent | Good | Good |
| Filtering | Advanced | Basic | Full SQL |
| Scale | Millions | Thousands | Millions |
| Persistence | Yes | Yes | Yes (Postgres) |
| Backups | Snapshots | File copy | pg_dump |
| Multi-tenancy | Collections | Collections | Schemas/tables |
| Existing infra | New service | New service | Reuses Postgres |
| License | Apache 2.0 | Apache 2.0 | PostgreSQL |

## HNSW Index Tuning

All three support HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) for fast approximate nearest neighbor search:

```python
# Qdrant HNSW config
client.create_collection(
    "docs",
    vectors_config=VectorParams(
        size=768,
        distance=Distance.COSINE,
    ),
    hnsw_config=HnswConfigDiff(
        m=16,               # Connections per node (higher = more memory, better recall)
        ef_construct=100,   # Build-time accuracy (higher = slower build, better quality)
        full_scan_threshold=10000  # Use exact search below this count
    )
)
```

## Embedding Dimensions by Model

| Embedding Model | Dimensions | Quality |
|----------------|------------|---------|
| nomic-embed-text | 768 | Excellent |
| all-MiniLM-L6-v2 | 384 | Good |
| all-mpnet-base-v2 | 768 | Excellent |
| mxbai-embed-large | 1024 | Best |
| text-embedding-ada-002 | 1536 | Excellent (OpenAI - cloud) |

For air-gapped: use nomic-embed-text (via Ollama) or all-MiniLM (via Transformers.js).

## Hardware Decision Framework

The central question: do you need GPU, or can CPU suffice?

CPU-only: Lower cost, simpler setup, adequate for batch processing and moderate traffic
GPU: Required for real-time interactive use with 7B+ models at acceptable latency

## Memory Math: How Much RAM/VRAM Do You Need?

```
Required memory = Model size (parameters * bytes per weight) + KV cache + OS overhead

Example: Llama 3.1 8B at Q4_K_M (4-bit):
- Model weights: 8B params * 0.5 bytes = 4.0 GB
- KV cache (8192 ctx): ~0.8 GB
- OS/framework overhead: ~0.5 GB
- Total: ~5.3 GB VRAM or RAM
```

## GPU Tiers for Enterprise

### Tier 1: Entry Production (7B models, 10-30 concurrent users)

| GPU | VRAM | Tokens/sec (7B Q4) | Cost (2025) |
|-----|------|--------------------|-------------|
| RTX 4060 Ti 16GB | 16 GB | 60-70 tok/s | ~$500 |
| RTX 4070 Ti Super | 16 GB | 70-85 tok/s | ~$800 |
| RTX 4080 | 16 GB | 80-100 tok/s | ~$1000 |

### Tier 2: Standard Production (13B-34B models)

| GPU | VRAM | Tokens/sec (13B Q4) | Cost (2025) |
|-----|------|--------------------|-------------|
| RTX 4090 | 24 GB | 50-70 tok/s | ~$2000 |
| RTX 3090 | 24 GB | 40-55 tok/s | ~$800 used |
| A10G | 24 GB | 40-60 tok/s | Cloud: $1.5/hr |
| L4 | 24 GB | 45-65 tok/s | Cloud: $0.8/hr |

### Tier 3: High-End (70B models, high throughput)

| GPU | VRAM | Tokens/sec (70B Q4) | Notes |
|-----|------|---------------------|-------|
| A100 40GB | 40 GB | 30-45 tok/s | Data center |
| A100 80GB | 80 GB | 40-60 tok/s | Full 70B + context |
| H100 80GB | 80 GB | 60-90 tok/s | Fastest option |
| 2x RTX 4090 | 48 GB | 35-50 tok/s | Consumer dual GPU |

### Apple Silicon (Best Price/Performance for CPU+GPU unified)

| Chip | Unified Memory | Tokens/sec (7B Q4) | Notes |
|------|---------------|---------------------|-------|
| M2 Pro 32GB | 32 GB | 40-55 tok/s | 13B fits |
| M3 Max 96GB | 96 GB | 70-90 tok/s | 70B fits |
| M3 Max 128GB | 128 GB | 75-95 tok/s | 70B + context |
| M4 Max 128GB | 128 GB | 90-120 tok/s | Best per watt |

Apple Silicon advantage: unified memory means GPU and CPU share RAM, so 96GB M3 Max can run 70B models that would need 2x A100 on traditional hardware.

## CPU-Only Deployment

Viable for: batch processing, low-concurrency use, small models (1B-7B), embeddings

```
Minimum production CPU server:
- CPU: AMD EPYC or Intel Xeon, 16+ cores
- RAM: 64 GB (for 7B Q4_K_M with headroom)
- Storage: NVMe SSD (model loading speed matters)
- Network: 1 Gbps internal

Performance expectation:
- 1B Q4: ~30-50 tok/s
- 3B Q4: ~15-25 tok/s
- 7B Q4: ~5-10 tok/s
- 13B Q4: ~3-6 tok/s
```

## Model Fit by Hardware

| Hardware | RAM/VRAM | Max Model (Q4_K_M) | Practical Model |
|----------|----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Laptop (8GB RAM) | 8 GB | 3B | 1B-3B |
| Workstation (32GB RAM) | 32 GB | 13B | 7B |
| Server (64GB RAM, CPU) | 64 GB | 34B | 13B-34B |
| RTX 4090 (24GB VRAM) | 24 GB | 13B | 7B-13B |
| 2x A100 (80GB VRAM) | 160 GB | 70B+ | 70B |
| M3 Max 128GB | 128 GB | 70B | 70B |

## Recommended Enterprise Configurations

### Configuration A: Small Team (5-20 users, 7B model)

```
- CPU: AMD Ryzen 9 7950X or Intel Core i9-14900K
- RAM: 64 GB DDR5
- GPU: NVIDIA RTX 4090 (24 GB) - runs 7B at 80 tok/s
- Storage: 2 TB NVMe (models + vector DB)
- OS: Ubuntu 22.04 LTS
- Stack: Ollama + AnythingLLM
- Cost: ~$4000-6000 one-time
```

### Configuration B: Medium Team (50-200 users, 13B-34B model)

```
- Server: Dell PowerEdge R750 or similar
- CPU: 2x AMD EPYC 7763 (128 cores)
- RAM: 256 GB DDR4 ECC
- GPU: 2x NVIDIA A100 40GB
- Storage: 10 TB NVMe RAID

- Stack: Ollama + AnythingLLM + Qdrant

```

### Configuration C: Enterprise (500+ users, 70B model, high availability)

```
- Multiple inference servers (load balanced)
- Each node: 8x H100 80GB
- Total VRAM: 640 GB per node
- Orchestration: Kubernetes
- Model: Llama 3.3 70B or Qwen 2.5 72B
- Stack: vLLM + custom gateway + Qdrant cluster
- Cost: $100,000+ per node
```

## Power and Cooling Considerations

## Model Selection Principles

Enterprise model selection involves: task requirements, hardware constraints, language needs, licensing, and update cadence. There is no single best model - the right choice depends on your specific use case.

## Top Open Models for Enterprise (2025)

### Llama 3.1 / 3.2 / 3.3 (Meta, Llama Community License)

```
Sizes: 1B, 3B, 8B, 70B, 405B
Strengths: General purpose, strong reasoning, excellent instruction following
Context: Up to 128K tokens (3.1/3.3), 8K (3.2 lightweight)
Languages: English primary, multilingual support
License: Llama Community License (commercial use allowed, restrictions apply)
GGUF names: Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct.Q4_K_M.gguf

Best for:
- General enterprise assistant (8B)
- Document Q&A (8B, 70B)
- Code generation (8B+)
- Long document processing (128K context, 70B)
```

### Mistral Small 3.1 (Mistral AI, Apache 2.0)

```
Size: 24B
Strengths: Excellent reasoning, strong multilingual, function calling
Context: 128K tokens
Languages: 11 languages including English, French, German, Spanish, Italian
License: Apache 2.0 (fully commercial friendly)
GGUF: Mistral-Small-3.1-24B-Instruct.Q4_K_M.gguf

Best for:
- Multilingual enterprise deployments
- Complex reasoning tasks
- When Apache 2.0 license is required
- Customer-facing applications (multiple languages)
```

### Phi 4 (Microsoft, MIT License)

```
Size: 14B
Strengths: Exceptional coding and reasoning for its size, very efficient
Context: 16K tokens
Languages: English primary
License: MIT (fully open, commercial friendly)
GGUF: Phi-4-Q4_K_M.gguf

Best for:
- Code review and generation
- Mathematical reasoning
- Resource-constrained hardware (14B punches above 70B in coding)
- Embedding in developer tools
```

### Qwen 2.5 (Alibaba, Apache 2.0)

```
Sizes: 0.5B, 1.5B, 3B, 7B, 14B, 32B, 72B
Strengths: Best multilingual, strong coding (Coder variants), long context
Context: 128K tokens
Languages: 29 languages (best non-English support)
License: Apache 2.0 (Qwen 2.5 and below)
GGUF: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct.Q4_K_M.gguf

Best for:
- Asian language enterprises (Chinese, Japanese, Korean)
- Multilingual document processing
- When Apache 2.0 is required
- Cost-sensitive deployments (0.5B-3B for simple tasks)
```

### DeepSeek R1 (DeepSeek, MIT License)

```
Sizes: 1.5B, 7B, 8B, 14B, 32B, 70B, 671B
Strengths: Chain-of-thought reasoning, math, complex analysis
Context: 64K tokens
Languages: English, Chinese
License: MIT
GGUF: DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B.Q4_K_M.gguf

Best for:
- Complex multi-step reasoning
- Financial analysis
- Legal document analysis
- Scientific/technical tasks
Note: R1 shows its reasoning chain explicitly - useful for auditability
```

### Gemma 2 (Google, Gemma Terms of Use)

```
Sizes: 2B, 9B, 27B
Strengths: Balanced quality, Google's training quality
Context: 8K tokens
Languages: English primary
License: Gemma Terms of Use (commercial use allowed with conditions)
GGUF: gemma-2-9b-it.Q4_K_M.gguf

Best for:
- Consumer-grade hardware
- General assistant tasks
- When model size is the constraint
```

## Task -> Model Recommendation Matrix

| Task | Recommended Model | Notes |
|------|-----------------|-------|
| General enterprise Q&A | Llama 3.1 8B Q4_K_M | Best balance |
| Document summarization | Llama 3.1 8B / Mistral 7B | 128K context helpful |
| Legal contract analysis | DeepSeek R1 7B / Llama 3.1 70B | Reasoning chain |
| Code review | Phi 4 14B | Best coding per parameter |
| Customer support | Qwen 2.5 7B | Multilingual |
| Financial analysis | DeepSeek R1 14B | Step-by-step reasoning |
| Multilingual docs | Mistral Small / Qwen 2.5 | 29+ languages |
| Embeddings | nomic-embed-text | Purpose-built |
| Fast classification | Llama 3.2 1B / Qwen 0.5B | Latency-first |
| Long docs (100K+) | Llama 3.1 70B (128K) | Full context |
| Air-gap constrained hardware | Phi 3.5 mini / Llama 3.2 3B | Small footprint |

## License Compatibility Guide

| Model Family | License | Commercial Use | Restrictions |
|-------------|---------|---------------|-------------|
| Llama 3.x | Llama Community | Yes | >700M MAU requires Meta license |
| Mistral | Apache 2.0 | Yes | None |
| Phi | MIT | Yes | None |
| Qwen 2.5 | Apache 2.0 | Yes | None |
| DeepSeek R1 | MIT | Yes | None |
| Gemma 2 | Gemma ToU | Yes | Attribution required |

For most enterprise use cases, Apache 2.0 and MIT are cleanest.
Llama license is acceptable for internal enterprise tools (not resale).

## Context Window Practical Limits

```
Rule of thumb: 1 page of text = ~500-800 tokens

Model context -> document processing capacity:
- 8K context = ~10-16 pages
- 16K context = ~20-32 pages
- 32K context = ~40-64 pages
- 128K context = ~160-256 pages (entire short book)

For large document RAG, chunk documents regardless of context size.
Use large context models only when full-document reasoning is needed.
```

## When WASM vs When Native Binary

WASM and native binary deployment serve fundamentally different use cases. Choosing wrong leads to poor performance, unnecessary complexity, or missed privacy opportunities.

## Decision Tree

```
Is the LLM running in a web browser?
  YES -> Must use WASM (WebLLM, wllama, Transformers.js)
  NO -> Continue...

Is this a desktop app (Electron, Tauri, macOS/Win/Linux)?
  YES -> Native binary preferred (Ollama, llama.cpp, LM Studio)
  NO -> Continue...

Is this a server/container deployment?
  YES -> Native binary preferred (Ollama, llama.cpp server, LocalAI)
  NO -> Continue...

Is this Node.js with no native binary allowed (edge, restricted env)?
  YES -> WASM via wllama or Transformers.js
  NO -> Use native binary for best performance
```

## Performance Comparison: WASM vs Native

| Runtime | 7B Q4_K_M | 1B Q4 | Notes |
|---------|-----------|-------|-------|
| Native (llama.cpp) | 80-120 tok/s | 200+ tok/s | Baseline |
| CUDA GPU (Ollama) | 80-100 tok/s | - | Near-native |
| Metal (Apple) | 70-90 tok/s | 150+ tok/s | Excellent |
| WASM native browser (WebGPU) | 30-60 tok/s | 80+ tok/s | 50-70% native |
| WASM CPU (wllama) | 5-15 tok/s | 30-50 tok/s | 20-30% native |
| Transformers.js WASM | 3-10 tok/s | 20-40 tok/s | Varies by model |

## WASM Advantages

- **Universal deployment**: Runs in any modern browser, no install needed
- **Sandboxed security**: WASM sandbox prevents filesystem/network access
- **Zero installation friction**: Users don't install anything
- **Client-side privacy**: Data stays in browser tab, even separate from server
- **Edge deployment**: Works in Cloudflare Workers, Deno Deploy
- **Cross-platform**: Same code runs on Windows, Mac, Linux, iOS, Android

## Native Binary Advantages

- **2-5x better performance**: Full CPU/GPU instruction sets
- **Larger models**: Not bound by browser memory limits
- **Multi-GPU**: Distribute model across multiple GPUs
- **Background processing**: Not limited by browser tab lifecycle
- **Production reliability**: Battle-tested server infrastructure
- **OS-level resource control**: Fine-tune threads, memory, NUMA

## WASM-Specific Constraints

```
Browser WASM:
- Memory: ~4GB per tab (practical limit for model loading)
- Threads: Requires Cross-Origin Isolation headers for SharedArrayBuffer
- Storage: 50MB+ models via IndexedDB (slow on first load)
- Context: Tab lifecycle (model lost when tab closes, unless cached)
- GPU: WebGPU (Chrome/Edge stable, others in progress)

Node.js WASM:
- Memory: Limited by Node.js heap (use --max-old-space-size=8192)
- Performance: No WebGPU, CPU only
- Thread: Worker threads for background processing
- Benefit: Same code as browser, no native compilation
```

## Hybrid Architecture: WASM for Edge, Native for Heavy

```javascript
// client-router.js
// Routes requests based on model size and available runtime

async function classify(text) {
  if (typeof window !== 'undefined' && navigator.gpu) {
    // Browser with GPU: use WebLLM for small models
    return await webLLMClassify(text, 'SmolLM2-1.7B');
  } else if (typeof window !== 'undefined') {
    // Browser CPU: use Transformers.js for classification
    return await transformersClassify(text);
  } else {
    // Server/Node.js: use Ollama for best performance
    return await ollamaClassify(text, 'llama3.2:1b');
  }
}

async function generateLong(prompt) {
  // Always use server-side for heavy generation
  return await ollamaGenerate(prompt, 'llama3.1:8b');
}
```

## Practical Deployment Scenarios

### Scenario A: Enterprise Internal Web App
- **Recommendation**: Ollama server (backend) + fetch API calls (frontend)
- **Why**: Best performance, simple architecture, works in all browsers
- **Privacy**: All data stays on corporate network

### Scenario B: Client-Facing SaaS with Privacy Guarantee
- **Recommendation**: WebLLM (browser) for sensitive queries + server for heavy tasks
- **Why**: Sensitive data never leaves client device
- **Privacy**: Medical, legal, personal data stays in browser

### Scenario C: Offline Desktop Tool (Electron)
- **Recommendation**: llama.cpp native bundled + Electron
- **Why**: Full native performance, bundled model
- **Privacy**: Completely air-gapped

### Scenario D: Edge/Serverless Function
- **Recommendation**: Transformers.js WASM or wllama
- **Why**: No native binary in Cloudflare Workers/Vercel Edge
- **Privacy**: Inference at edge, data never reaches origin

### Scenario E: Mobile Web (iOS/Android Browser)
- **Recommendation**: Transformers.js with small models (< 500MB)
- **Why**: WebGPU on mobile is limited, WASM is universal
- **Privacy**: On-device processing
kb_352:
## Performance Levers for Local LLM

Local LLM performance has two axes: throughput (tokens per second) and latency (time to first token). Different enterprise use cases prioritize differently:

- **Interactive chat**: Low latency matters most (< 1s TTFT)
- **Document batch processing**: Throughput matters (max tok/s)
- **API with many users**: Both matter (continuous batching)

## GPU Layer Offloading (Most Impactful)

```bash
# Every layer offloaded to GPU = significantly faster
# Calculate: model has N layers, each needs X MB VRAM

# Llama 3.1 8B Q4_K_M: 32 transformer layers, ~125 MB each
# RTX 4090 (24GB): offload all 32 layers
llama-server --model llama3.1-8b.Q4_K_M.gguf --n-gpu-layers 99  # 99 = all

# 8GB VRAM: offload ~20 layers (rest on CPU)
llama-server --model llama3.1-8b.Q4_K_M.gguf --n-gpu-layers 20
# Result: ~3x slower than full GPU, but 3x faster than pure CPU

# Monitor GPU usage
nvidia-smi --query-gpu=utilization.gpu,memory.used --format=csv -l 1
```

## Flash Attention

```bash
# Flash attention reduces VRAM by ~50% and speeds up long-context inference
llama-server --model model.gguf --flash-attn

# With flash attention: 8B model can fit in 6GB VRAM at 8K context
# Without: needs 8GB VRAM for same

# Ollama enables flash attention automatically when supported
# llama.cpp: pass --flash-attn flag
```

## Continuous Batching for Multi-User

```bash
# Without continuous batching: requests queue, one at a time
# With continuous batching: new requests fill empty decode slots

llama-server \
  --model model.gguf \
  --parallel 4 \          # 4 simultaneous decode slots
  --cont-batching \         # Enable continuous batching
  --ctx-size 32768          # Total context for all slots (32768/4 = 8192 per slot)

# Result: 4x throughput under concurrent load
# Check slots: curl http://localhost:8080/slots
```

## Context Size vs Performance

```
Context size affects BOTH memory and speed:

8B model, RTX 4090 (24GB):
- ctx 2048:  ~90 tok/s, needs 5.5 GB
- ctx 8192:  ~75 tok/s, needs 7.0 GB
- ctx 32768: ~55 tok/s, needs 12 GB
- ctx 131072: ~25 tok/s, needs ~22 GB

Use the minimum context your use case requires.
```

## KV Cache Quantization

```bash
# Quantize the KV cache to reduce VRAM at minimal quality cost
llama-server \
  --model model.gguf \
  --cache-type-k q8_0 \    # 8-bit KV cache (was 16-bit)
  --cache-type-v q8_0       # Reduces KV cache VRAM by ~50%
```

## CPU Thread Tuning

```bash
# For CPU layers (partial GPU offload or CPU-only)
# Optimal threads = physical cores (not hyperthreaded)

# Check physical cores
lscpu | grep "Core(s) per socket"

# Set threads explicitly
llama-server --model model.gguf --threads 8 --threads-batch 8

# Ollama environment variable
OLLAMA_NUM_CPU_THREADS=8 ollama serve
```

## mmap vs RAM Loading

```bash
# Memory-mapped (default): model file stays on disk, OS pages in as needed
# Pros: fast startup, lower RAM peak, OS handles caching
# Cons: disk I/O on first load, inconsistent latency

# RAM loading: entire model loaded into RAM
llama-server --model model.gguf --no-mmap
# Pros: consistent latency, no disk I/O after startup
# Cons: higher peak RAM, slower startup

# Enterprise recommendation: use --no-mmap for predictable latency on NVMe SSD servers
```

## Benchmarking Your Setup

```bash
# llama.cpp built-in benchmark
llama-bench \
  --model model.Q4_K_M.gguf \
  --prompt 512 \           # Prompt tokens (prefill)
  --n-gen 256 \            # Generation tokens
  --n-gpu-layers 99 \
  --threads 8

# Output shows:
# pp (prompt processing) = tokens/sec for prompt evaluation
# tg (text generation) = tokens/sec for generation
# Both on GPU and CPU layers

# Ollama benchmark
ollama run llama3.1:8b --verbose "Write a 500 word essay" 2>&1 | tail -20
# Shows: eval rate (generation speed)
```

## Throughput vs Latency Trade-offs

| Setting | Throughput | Latency | Use Case |
|---------|-----------|---------|----------|
| High ctx (128K) | Lower | Higher | Document analysis |
| Low ctx (2K) | Higher | Lower | Simple Q&A |
| Many parallel slots | Higher | Higher (per user) | Batch API |
| Single slot | Lower | Lowest | Interactive chat |
| Full GPU offload | Highest | Lowest | Best hardware |
| CPU only | Lowest | Highest | No GPU available |

## Prompt Caching

```python
# Ollama supports KV cache reuse for repeated prefixes
# If system prompt is reused across requests, it is cached
# Subsequent requests with same prefix skip re-evaluation

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='llama3.1:8b',
    messages=[
        {'role': 'system', 'content': LONG_SYSTEM_PROMPT},  # Cached after 1st use
        {'role': 'user', 'content': user_question}
    ]
)
# First request: processes full system prompt
# Subsequent requests: system prompt from cache (instant)
```

## Docker Production Deployment

### Ollama Production Docker

```yaml
# docker-compose.production.yml
version: '3.8'
services:
  ollama:
    image: ollama/ollama:latest
    ports:
      - "11434:11434"
    volumes:
      - /data/ollama-models:/root/.ollama
    environment:
      - OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0
      - OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL=8
      - OLLAMA_MAX_LOADED_MODELS=3
      - OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=30m
      - OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=1
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 80G
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: 1
              capabilities: [gpu]
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:11434/api/version"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 10s
      retries: 3
    restart: unless-stopped
    logging:
      driver: "json-file"
      options:
        max-size: "100m"
        max-file: "3"
```

### nginx Load Balancer for Multiple Ollama Instances

```nginx
# nginx.conf for Ollama cluster
upstream ollama_cluster {
  least_conn;  # Route to least-loaded server
  server ollama-1:11434;
  server ollama-2:11434;
  server ollama-3:11434;
  keepalive 32;
}

server {
  listen 11434;

  location / {
    proxy_pass http://ollama_cluster;
    proxy_http_version 1.1;
    proxy_set_header Connection "";
    proxy_buffering off;           # Critical for streaming
    proxy_read_timeout 300s;
    proxy_send_timeout 300s;

    # Authentication
    auth_basic "Private AI";
    auth_basic_user_file /etc/nginx/.htpasswd;
  }
}
```

## Kubernetes Deployment

```yaml
# ollama-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ollama
  namespace: ai-private
spec:
  replicas: 2  # Multiple replicas for HA
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ollama
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ollama
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: ollama
          image: ollama/ollama:latest
          ports:
            - containerPort: 11434
          env:
            - name: OLLAMA_NUM_PARALLEL
              value: "4"
            - name: OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE
              value: "30m"
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "16Gi"
              nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
            limits:
              memory: "32Gi"
              nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
          volumeMounts:
            - name: models
              mountPath: /root/.ollama
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /api/version
              port: 11434
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 30
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /api/tags
              port: 11434
            initialDelaySeconds: 60
            periodSeconds: 10
      volumes:
        - name: models
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: ollama-models-pvc  # Pre-populated with models
      nodeSelector:
        gpu: "true"
      tolerations:
        - key: "nvidia.com/gpu"
          operator: "Exists"
          effect: "NoSchedule"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: ollama
  namespace: ai-private
spec:
  selector:
    app: ollama
  ports:
    - port: 11434
      targetPort: 11434
  type: ClusterIP  # Internal only - no external exposure
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: ollama-models-pvc
  namespace: ai-private
spec:
  accessModes: [ReadWriteMany]  # Shared across replicas
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 200Gi
  storageClassName: nfs-fast  # NFS for shared model storage
```

## Model Pre-Population Init Container

```yaml
# Init container to pre-pull models before serving traffic
initContainers:
  - name: model-loader
    image: ollama/ollama:latest
    command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
    args:
      - |
        ollama serve &
        sleep 5
        ollama pull llama3.1:8b
        ollama pull nomic-embed-text
        ollama pull phi4
        kill %1
    volumeMounts:
      - name: models
        mountPath: /root/.ollama
```

## Horizontal Pod Autoscaler

```yaml

kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: ollama-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1

  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 8
```

## API Gateway with Rate Limiting

```yaml
# Kong or Traefik ingress with rate limiting
apiVersion: traefik.io/v1alpha1
kind: Middleware
metadata:
  name: ai-rate-limit
spec:
  rateLimit:
    average: 60      # 60 requests per minute
    burst: 10        # Allow burst of 10
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: ai-ingress
  annotations:
    traefik.ingress.kubernetes.io/router.middlewares: ai-rate-limit@kubernetescrd
spec:
  rules:
    - host: ai.internal.corp.net
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /v1
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: ollama
                port:
                  number: 11434
```

## Monitoring with Prometheus

```yaml
# Prometheus scrape config for Ollama metrics
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'ollama'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['ollama:11434']
    metrics_path: '/metrics'
    scrape_interval: 15s
```

```promql
# Key metrics to monitor
# Request rate
rate(ollama_request_total[5m])

# Token generation rate
rate(ollama_tokens_total[5m])

# Model load time
ollama_model_load_duration_seconds

# Queue depth (waiting requests)
ollama_pending_requests_total

# GPU utilization (via nvidia-smi exporter)
DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL
```

# What Is the RuvNet Ecosystem? A Complete Map of Every Tool and How They Connect

## Why Should You Care?

You have heard the names: RuVector, AgentDB, Claude Flow, Agentic Flow, SONA, AIMDS, ReasoningBank, ruv-swarm, Flow Nexus, RVF. That is ten major tools. Each one does something important, but the real power comes from how they connect. Understanding the ecosystem as a whole is the difference between "I have a bunch of separate tools" and "I have an integrated AI system that thinks, learns, remembers, and protects itself."

This document is the master map. Read it first, then dive into any individual tool that interests you.

## The Factory Analogy

Think of the RuvNet ecosystem as an automated factory. Every factory needs raw materials, workers, managers, memory, security, and a way to ship products. Here is how each tool maps to a factory role:

- **RuVector** is the warehouse. It stores and retrieves information at incredible speed using vector search -- finding things by meaning, not just keywords.
- **AgentDB** is the filing system inside the warehouse. It gives AI agents structured memory so they remember what they learned yesterday.
- **Claude Flow** is the floor manager. It orchestrates which agents work on which tasks, coordinates handoffs, and makes sure nothing falls through the cracks.
- **Agentic Flow** is the individual worker. It is the runtime that actually executes tasks -- reading code, writing files, running tests.
- **SONA** is the efficiency consultant. It watches everything in real time and optimizes routing, deciding which worker and which tools handle each task in under 0.05 milliseconds.
- **ReasoningBank** is the training manual library. It stores every successful approach the factory has ever used, indexed by meaning so workers can find relevant past experience instantly.
- **AIMDS** is the security system. It scans every input and output for attacks, learns from each incident, and gets better without manual updates.
- **ruv-swarm** is the team coordination system. It lets multiple agents work together in formations -- star, mesh, pipeline, or hierarchy -- depending on the task.
- **Flow Nexus** is the shipping dock. It deploys your AI agents to the cloud with sandboxed execution, an app store, and real-time streaming.
- **RVF** is the shipping container. A cognitive container format with 24 segment types that packages vectors, indexes, WASM code, and metadata into a single 5.5KB file that self-boots in 125 milliseconds.

## How a Query Flows Through the System

Here is what happens when you ask your AI system a question, step by step:

1. **Input arrives** -- your question enters through Claude Flow or Flow Nexus.
2. **AIMDS scans it** -- in under 5 milliseconds, the security layer checks for prompt injection, jailbreaks, and data leaks.
3. **SONA routes it** -- in 0.05 milliseconds, SONA decides which model (Haiku for simple tasks, Opus for complex ones) and which agent type should handle this.
4. **Claude Flow assigns it** -- the orchestrator picks the right agent or spawns a swarm if the task is complex.
5. **ruv-swarm coordinates** -- if multiple agents are needed, ruv-swarm organizes them into the optimal topology.
6. **Agentic Flow executes** -- the agent runtime does the actual work: reading files, writing code, running commands.
7. **ReasoningBank advises** -- the agent searches past reasoning patterns to find how similar tasks were handled before, achieving 90%+ success rates.
8. **AgentDB remembers** -- results, learnings, and experiences are stored as structured memory with vector embeddings.
9. **RuVector indexes** -- all memory is indexed using HNSW for sub-millisecond semantic retrieval.
10. **AIMDS scans the output** -- before the response reaches you, it is checked for data leaks and sensitive information.

## The Three Layers

The ecosystem organizes into three layers:

**Core Layer** (the foundation):
- RuVector-Core: HNSW indexing, SIMD acceleration, quantization, zero-copy I/O. Under 0.5ms per query, 95%+ recall.
- RVF: The cognitive container format. 24 segment types, 5.5KB WASM, boots in 125ms.

**Intelligence Layer** (the brain):
- SONA: Two-Tier LoRA + EWC++ + ReasoningBank. Under 1ms real-time learning.
- AgentDB: 6 cognitive memory patterns, 25 latent space simulations, GNN 8-head attention.
- ReasoningBank: RETRIEVE-JUDGE-DISTILL-CONSOLIDATE pipeline. 32.6 million operations per second.
- Agentic Flow: Agent Booster (352x faster), Multi-Model Router (85-99% cost savings).

**Orchestration Layer** (the coordination):
- Claude Flow: 96 MCP tools, SPARC framework, 36 skills.
- ruv-swarm: Star, mesh, pipeline, hierarchy topologies with auto-scaling.
- Flow Nexus: Cloud deployment, E2B sandboxes, app store, real-time streaming.
- AIMDS: Three-layer security with self-learning.

## How It Connects

Every tool connects to at least two others:
- RuVector powers the vector search inside AgentDB, AIMDS, and ReasoningBank.
- ReasoningBank feeds learned patterns into both SONA and AIMDS.
- Claude Flow orchestrates agents that run on Agentic Flow.
- ruv-swarm coordinates multi-agent tasks assigned by Claude Flow.
- Flow Nexus deploys the whole stack to the cloud.

## Key Numbers

| Component | Key Metric |
|-----------|------------|
| RuVector | 150x-12,500x faster than brute-force vector search |
| AgentDB | 150x faster than SQLite, 32 MCP tools, 59 CLI commands |
| Claude Flow | 96 MCP tools, 36 skills |
| Agentic Flow | 352x faster code edits, 85-99% cost savings |
| SONA | 0.05ms routing decisions |
| ReasoningBank | 32.6M operations/sec, 46% faster execution |
| AIMDS | Under 10ms full detection, 50+ attack patterns |
| ruv-swarm | 4 topology types, 3-5x speedup |
| Flow Nexus | 70+ MCP tools, cloud sandboxes |
| RVF | 5.5KB WASM, 125ms boot, 24 segment types |

## Why This Matters

Most AI systems are a collection of disconnected tools. The RuvNet ecosystem is different because every component was designed to work with every other component. RuVector provides the vector backbone. AgentDB provides the memory. SONA provides the optimization. ReasoningBank provides the learning. AIMDS provides the security. Claude Flow provides the orchestration. And Flow Nexus provides the deployment.

The result is an AI system that gets faster, smarter, and more secure every time it runs -- without any manual tuning.

# What Is RuVector? The Vector Database Built for AI Agent Memory

## Why Should You Care?

Every AI system needs to store and find information. Traditional databases store rows and columns -- great for names and numbers, terrible for meaning. When you ask "find documents about improving code quality," a traditional database looks for those exact words. A vector database like RuVector finds documents about code quality even when they use completely different words like "refactoring best practices" or "clean code techniques."

RuVector is the vector search engine that powers the entire RuvNet ecosystem. It is the foundation that AgentDB, AIMDS, ReasoningBank, and SONA all rely on for fast, meaning-based retrieval.

## The Highway System Analogy

Think of finding information like driving to a destination in a large city. Traditional search is like following street addresses -- you need the exact address or you get lost. Keyword search is like asking for directions using landmarks -- better, but still limited.

RuVector uses HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) indexing, which works like a highway system. At the top level, you have a few major highways connecting distant parts of the city. Each level down adds more roads. At the bottom, you have every local street. When you search for something, HNSW starts on the highway (fast, approximate), narrows down to local roads (precise, exact), and finds your destination in under 0.5 milliseconds. No matter how large the city grows, the highway system keeps working.

## How RuVector Differs from pgvector

You might have heard of pgvector, the popular PostgreSQL extension for vector search. RuVector and pgvector both add vector capabilities to PostgreSQL, but they are built for different purposes:

**pgvector** is a general-purpose vector extension. It stores vectors and does similarity search. It works well for basic use cases.

**RuVector** is purpose-built for AI agent workloads. The key differences:

- **Custom HNSW implementation**: RuVector uses a Rust-based HNSW engine with SIMD acceleration and quantization. This delivers 150x to 12,500x speed improvements over brute-force search, compared to pgvector's IVFFlat and HNSW indexes which are slower on equivalent hardware.
- **WASM support**: RuVector compiles to WebAssembly, so it runs in browsers, edge devices, and serverless functions. pgvector is PostgreSQL-only.
- **Agent-native design**: RuVector includes cognitive memory patterns (Reflexion, Skills, Causal Memory, Explainable Recall, Utility Ranking) built into the search layer. pgvector is a raw vector store with no agent awareness.
- **Zero-copy I/O**: RuVector avoids memory copies during search, reducing latency and memory usage.
- **GNN attention**: RuVector includes Graph Neural Network 8-head attention for learned edge importance weighting, improving recall by 1.6% while being 38.8% faster.

## Core Functions

The two functions you will encounter most:

**ruvector_embed(text)**: Converts text into a vector embedding. Under the hood, it uses ONNX-based models (MiniLM-L6-v2 at 384 dimensions for speed, or bge-base-en-v1.5 at 768 dimensions for production accuracy). All embedding runs locally -- no external API calls.

**Cosine similarity (<=>)**: Compares two vectors and returns how similar they are. A score of 1.0 means identical meaning; 0.0 means completely unrelated. RuVector's HNSW index makes this comparison across millions of vectors in sub-millisecond time.

## How It Connects

RuVector is the foundation layer that other tools build on:

- **AgentDB** uses RuVector for all its semantic memory storage and retrieval -- every pattern, every learned experience is a vector in RuVector.
- **AIMDS** uses RuVector's HNSW search to find semantically similar past attacks, even when attackers rephrase their prompts.
- **ReasoningBank** indexes all reasoning patterns through RuVector for instant retrieval at 32.6 million operations per second.
- **SONA** relies on RuVector embeddings to represent task characteristics for routing decisions.
- **RVF** containers use RuVector's indexing format to store pre-built indexes that boot in 125 milliseconds.

## Key Numbers

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Speed advantage over brute-force | 150x - 12,500x |
| p50 query latency | < 0.5ms |
| Recall accuracy | 95%+ |
| WASM binary size | 5.5KB |
| Boot time (RVF container) | 125ms |
| GNN attention heads | 8 |
| GNN recall improvement | +1.6% |
| GNN speed improvement | +38.8% |
| Embedding dimensions (fast) | 384 (MiniLM-L6-v2) |
| Embedding dimensions (production) | 768 (bge-base-en-v1.5) |

## Why This Matters

Without RuVector, every other tool in the ecosystem would need to either use slow brute-force search or depend on an external cloud service. RuVector keeps everything local, fast, and under your control. It is the reason the entire stack can run on a single machine while delivering performance that rivals cloud-hosted vector databases costing thousands of dollars per month.

# What Is AgentDB? Persistent Memory That Makes AI Agents Remember

## Why Should You Care?

Most AI agents have amnesia. They handle a task, do brilliant work, and then forget everything the moment the session ends. The next time you ask the same question, they start from scratch. No memory of what worked, no memory of what failed, no accumulation of expertise.

AgentDB fixes this. It is a persistent memory system that gives AI agents the ability to remember experiences, learn from mistakes, and build expertise over time -- just like a human employee who gets better at their job with each passing month.

## The Institutional Memory Analogy

Think of a company where employees keep detailed notes about every project. When a new project comes in, they check the filing system: "Have we done something like this before? What worked? What did not?" Over years, the company builds institutional memory that makes it smarter than any individual employee.

AgentDB is that filing system for AI agents. It stores structured memories -- not just raw text, but organized knowledge about what the agent did, why it did it, whether it worked, and what it learned.

## The Six Cognitive Memory Patterns

AgentDB is not a simple key-value store. It implements six sophisticated memory patterns inspired by cognitive science and machine learning:

1. **Reflexion Self-Critique**: After completing a task, the agent reviews its own performance. "Did I make the right choices? What could I have done better?" These self-evaluations are stored and retrieved when similar tasks appear later.

2. **Skills Memory**: When an agent develops a reusable approach -- a code pattern, a debugging technique, a configuration trick -- it stores that as a reusable skill. Next time a similar problem appears, the skill is retrieved and applied directly.

3. **Causal Memory**: Records cause-and-effect relationships. "When I changed configuration X, it broke service Y." This prevents agents from repeating mistakes and helps them understand system dependencies.

4. **Explainable Recall with Merkle Proofs**: Every memory retrieval includes a verifiable chain of evidence. You can trace exactly WHY the agent made a decision and which past experiences influenced it. Merkle proofs provide cryptographic verification that the memory has not been tampered with.

5. **Utility Ranking**: Not all memories are equally useful. AgentDB tracks how often each memory is retrieved and how helpful it was. High-utility memories float to the top; rarely-used memories sink. Over time, the system naturally surfaces the most valuable knowledge.

6. **Nightly Learner**: A background process that consolidates and optimizes stored memories. Like the human brain during sleep, it strengthens important connections, prunes redundant data, and compresses old memories to save space.

## How It Works with RuVector

AgentDB stores memories as structured records, but the magic is in retrieval. Every memory is converted into a vector embedding using RuVector and indexed with HNSW. When an agent needs to recall relevant experience, it does not search by keywords -- it searches by meaning.

Ask "how to handle a failing database connection" and AgentDB finds memories about connection pooling, retry logic, timeout configuration, and circuit breakers -- even if none of those memories contain the word "failing."

This semantic retrieval runs at 150x the speed of traditional SQLite-based memory, which is critical when agents need to check their memory thousands of times during a complex task.

## The 25 Latent Space Simulations

AgentDB includes 25 simulation scenarios that test and validate memory operations. These include HNSW exploration (verifying vector search accuracy), GNN attention testing (ensuring the graph neural network correctly weights memory connections), and self-healing validation (confirming the system can recover from data corruption).

## How It Connects

- **RuVector**: Provides the vector indexing engine for all semantic memory operations.
- **ReasoningBank**: Stores reasoning patterns that AgentDB's cognitive memory retrieves during task execution.
- **Claude Flow**: Uses AgentDB as the institutional memory for all agents it orchestrates.
- **Agentic Flow**: The runtime that reads from and writes to AgentDB during task execution.
- **AIMDS**: Uses AgentDB's memory patterns to track and learn from security incidents.

## Key Numbers

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Speed vs SQLite | 150x faster |
| MCP tools | 32 |
| CLI commands | 59 |
| Cognitive memory patterns | 6 |
| Latent space simulations | 25 |
| GNN attention heads | 8 |
| GNN recall improvement | +12.4% |
| GNN forward pass | 3.8ms |
| Self-healing accuracy | 97.9% |
| ReasoningBank throughput | 32.6M ops/sec |

## Why This Matters

An AI agent without memory is like an employee with amnesia -- brilliant but unable to build on past work. AgentDB transforms AI agents from stateless question-answering machines into learning systems that accumulate expertise. Every task they complete makes them better at the next one. That is the difference between a tool you use and a team member who grows.
kb_358:
# What Is Agentic Flow? The Runtime That Lets AI Agents Think and Act

## Why Should You Care?

Claude Flow tells agents WHAT to do. Agentic Flow is HOW they do it. It is the execution engine -- the runtime environment where AI agents actually read files, write code, run tests, and make decisions. Without Agentic Flow, your agents would have instructions but no hands.

Think of it this way: Claude Flow is the project manager who assigns tasks. Agentic Flow is the skilled worker who picks up the tools and builds things. Both are essential, but they do very different jobs.

## The Workshop Analogy

Imagine a workshop where craftspeople build furniture. The workshop itself -- the workbench, the power tools, the safety equipment, the lighting -- is what Agentic Flow provides. It does not decide what to build (that is Claude Flow's job). It provides the environment and tools that make building possible, fast, and safe.

A bad workshop slows everything down. Dull tools, poor lighting, no organization. A great workshop makes the craftsperson 10x more productive. Agentic Flow is the great workshop.

## How It Differs from Claude Flow

This is the most common point of confusion, so here is the clear distinction:

**Claude Flow** is the orchestrator. It decides which agents to spawn, assigns tasks, coordinates handoffs between agents, manages the SPARC framework, and provides 96 MCP tools for integration. Claude Flow answers: "Who should do this work, and in what order?"

**Agentic Flow** is the executor. It provides the runtime where individual agents actually perform work. It includes the Agent Booster for fast code operations, the Multi-Model Router for cost optimization, ReasoningBank for learning, and QUIC Transport for fast communication. Agentic Flow answers: "How do I do this work as fast and cheaply as possible?"

They work together: Claude Flow assigns a coding task to an agent. That agent runs inside Agentic Flow, which provides the 352x-faster code editing, the cost-optimized model selection, and the learned reasoning patterns.

## Core Components

### Agent Booster: 352x Faster Code Operations

Traditional AI coding agents wait 352 milliseconds between each code edit because they make an API call for every change. Agentic Flow's Agent Booster uses Rust/WASM-powered local transformations that execute in 1 millisecond. For a session with 100 edits, that is 35 seconds versus 0.1 seconds. For 1,000 files, it is 5.87 minutes versus 1 second. And local operations cost nothing -- zero API calls means zero cost.

### Multi-Model Router: 85-99% Cost Savings

Not every task needs the most powerful (and expensive) AI model. The Multi-Model Router analyzes each task and routes it to the most cost-effective model that can handle it. Simple formatting? Use Haiku. Complex architecture? Use Opus. This routing saves 85-99% on API costs without sacrificing quality where it matters.

### ReasoningBank Integration

Agentic Flow integrates directly with ReasoningBank, which means agents learn from every task. First attempt at a new task type: 70% success rate. After learning from a few examples: 90%+ success rate and 46% faster execution. The agent does not just repeat -- it improves.

### QUIC Transport: 50-70% Faster Communication

When agents need to communicate with each other or with external services, Agentic Flow uses QUIC transport (the protocol behind HTTP/3). This delivers 50-70% lower latency than TCP, which matters when agents are exchanging thousands of messages during a complex task.

### Federation Hub: Infinite Scale

For enterprise workloads, the Federation Hub creates ephemeral agents with 5-second to 15-minute lifetimes and persistent cross-agent memory. Agents spin up, do their work, share what they learned, and shut down. The knowledge persists even after the agent is gone.

## How It Connects

- **Claude Flow**: Assigns tasks to agents that run inside Agentic Flow. Claude Flow orchestrates; Agentic Flow executes.
- **AgentDB**: Agentic Flow reads from and writes to AgentDB during every task, building persistent memory.
- **RuVector**: All vector operations within Agentic Flow use RuVector's HNSW engine.
- **ReasoningBank**: Agents consult ReasoningBank before every significant decision and store new patterns after completion.
- **SONA**: Optimizes Agentic Flow's model selection and routing in real time.
- **ruv-swarm**: When Claude Flow assigns a multi-agent task, ruv-swarm coordinates the agents while each one runs in Agentic Flow.

## Key Numbers

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Code edit speedup | 352x (352ms to 1ms) |
| 100 edits | 0.1 seconds (vs 35 seconds) |
| 1,000 files | 1 second (vs 5.87 minutes) |
| Cost per local edit | $0.00 |
| Cost savings (router) | 85-99% |
| Learning improvement | 70% to 90%+ success |
| Execution speedup after learning | 46% faster |
| QUIC vs TCP latency | 50-70% lower |
| Federation agent lifetime | 5s - 15min |

## Why This Matters

Speed and cost are the two biggest barriers to using AI agents in production. Agentic Flow attacks both simultaneously. The 352x code edit speedup means agents can handle large codebases without frustrating delays. The 85-99% cost savings mean you can run agents continuously without worrying about API bills. And the learning system means agents get better over time instead of making the same mistakes forever.
kb_359:
# What Is ruv-swarm? Running Multiple AI Agents as a Team

## Why Should You Care?

A single AI agent can do impressive work. But some tasks are too big, too complex, or too time-sensitive for one agent alone. Writing a full application requires a coder, a tester, a reviewer, and a documenter working together. Analyzing a large codebase requires many eyes scanning different modules simultaneously. Debugging a distributed system requires agents that can investigate multiple services at once.

ruv-swarm is the coordination layer that turns individual AI agents into a team. It manages how agents communicate, share work, and combine their results -- just like a well-run team of human specialists.

## The Sports Team Analogy

Think of a basketball team. Five players on the court, each with a position and role. The point guard calls plays (orchestration). The center handles rebounds (heavy lifting). The shooting guard scores from distance (specialized skill). They pass the ball, set screens, and communicate constantly.

ruv-swarm is the playbook and communication system for AI agent teams. It defines who does what, how they pass information, and how their individual contributions combine into a result that no single player could achieve alone.

## The Four Topologies

The key insight behind ruv-swarm is that different tasks need different team structures. You would not organize a basketball team the same way you organize an assembly line. ruv-swarm offers four topologies:

### Star Topology
One central coordinator, multiple specialist workers. The coordinator receives the task, breaks it into subtasks, assigns each subtask to a specialist, and combines the results.

**Best for**: Tasks that decompose cleanly into independent parts. Example: "Review this codebase" -- the coordinator assigns each module to a different reviewer, then merges their findings.

### Mesh Topology
Every agent can communicate with every other agent. No central coordinator. Agents negotiate, share discoveries, and self-organize.

**Best for**: Exploratory tasks where discoveries change the plan. Example: "Debug this production issue" -- agents investigating logs, code, and configs share clues with each other as they find them.

### Pipeline Topology
Agents are arranged in sequence. Agent A's output becomes Agent B's input, which becomes Agent C's input. Like an assembly line.

**Best for**: Tasks with clear sequential stages. Example: "Write, test, and deploy this feature" -- a coder writes it, a tester validates it, a deployer ships it.

### Hierarchy Topology
Multiple levels of coordination. A top-level manager delegates to mid-level leads, who delegate to individual workers. Reports flow back up the chain.

**Best for**: Very large tasks that need both breadth and depth. Example: "Refactor the entire application" -- the architect plans the structure, team leads manage each module, coders implement changes.

## How It Works Under the Hood

ruv-swarm is built on high-performance WebAssembly. The npm package (ruv-swarm, version 1.0.20) includes both a CLI (`ruv-swarm` command) and a programmatic API. Under the hood:

1. **Cognitive Profiles**: Each agent in a swarm has a cognitive profile describing its specializations (JavaScript: 0.95, React: 0.88, Testing: 0.82). ruv-swarm uses these profiles to assign tasks to the best-qualified agent.

2. **Auto-scaling**: The swarm grows or shrinks based on workload. If a task turns out to be larger than expected, ruv-swarm spawns additional agents. When subtasks complete, idle agents are released.

3. **Persistence and Monitoring**: All agent activities are logged and persisted. You can inspect what each agent did, how long it took, and what it produced.

4. **Self-Learning Topology Selection**: Over time, ruv-swarm learns which topology works best for which type of task. It starts with a reasonable default and improves with experience.

## How It Connects

- **Claude Flow**: Assigns multi-agent tasks to ruv-swarm. Claude Flow decides THAT a swarm is needed; ruv-swarm decides HOW to organize it.
- **Agentic Flow**: Each agent in the swarm runs inside Agentic Flow, using its Agent Booster, Multi-Model Router, and ReasoningBank.
- **AgentDB**: Swarm results and learnings are stored in AgentDB, so future swarms benefit from past experience.
- **SONA**: Helps optimize agent-to-task assignment within the swarm based on past performance data.
- **Flow Nexus**: Swarms can be deployed to the cloud through Flow Nexus for distributed execution.

## Key Numbers

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Topology types | 4 (star, mesh, pipeline, hierarchy) |
| Speedup vs single agent | 3-5x |
| Package version | 1.0.20 |
| Runtime | WebAssembly |
| Features | Persistence, monitoring, auto-scaling |
| Agent specialization tracking | Per-skill confidence scores |
| Self-learning | Topology selection improves with use |

## Why This Matters

The hardest problems in software and AI are not single-agent problems. They require coordination, specialization, and parallel execution. ruv-swarm makes multi-agent collaboration practical by handling the coordination complexity so you can focus on the task. Instead of manually splitting work and merging results, you describe the task and let ruv-swarm figure out the team structure.
kb_360:
# What Is Flow Nexus? Deploying Your AI Agents to the Cloud

## Why Should You Care?

Building AI agents on your local machine is powerful. But what happens when you want agents running 24/7, available to your team, or handling tasks while you sleep? You need to deploy them to the cloud. Flow Nexus is the platform that takes your local AI agent setup and runs it in the cloud with sandboxed execution, an app store for sharing agents, and real-time streaming of results.

Think of the difference between cooking at home and opening a restaurant. The recipes are the same, but the restaurant needs a commercial kitchen, a delivery system, a menu, and the ability to serve many customers at once. Flow Nexus is the restaurant infrastructure for your AI agents.

## The Restaurant Analogy

- **Kitchen** (Sandbox Execution): Each agent runs in its own isolated sandbox with its own environment, packages, and timeout. If one agent crashes, it does not affect any other. Just like separate cooking stations in a commercial kitchen.
- **Menu** (App Store): Published agents are listed in the app store with descriptions, capabilities, and usage stats. Other users can discover and deploy your agents.
- **Ordering System** (Workflows): Customers place orders (tasks), which are routed to the right kitchen station (agent), prepared, and delivered. Complex orders trigger multi-step workflows.
- **Delivery** (Real-Time Streaming): Results stream back to the customer in real time, so they can see progress instead of waiting for everything to finish.

## Core Capabilities

### Sandbox Execution (E2B Integration)

Every agent task runs inside an E2B sandbox -- a fully isolated execution environment with its own operating system, file system, and packages. Sandboxes are created on demand, run for up to one hour, and are destroyed when done. This means:

- Agents can install any packages they need without affecting your system.
- Untrusted code runs safely -- a malicious script cannot escape the sandbox.
- Each sandbox is reproducible -- same environment, same results, every time.

You manage sandboxes with simple commands: create, execute, configure, upload files, check logs, and delete.

### Workflows and Task Orchestration

Flow Nexus supports multi-step workflows where tasks flow through a sequence of agents. A typical workflow might be: (1) gather data, (2) analyze it, (3) generate a report, (4) send notifications. Each step can use a different agent with different capabilities.

Workflows include audit trails that record every step, every decision, and every result. You can trace exactly what happened and why.

### App Store

Built agents can be published to the Flow Nexus app store. Other users can search for, install, and run your agents. Each app includes analytics: how many users, how often it runs, and satisfaction scores.

### Real-Time Streaming

When an agent is working on a long task, Flow Nexus streams progress updates back to you in real time. You subscribe to an execution stream and receive updates as they happen, rather than waiting for the entire task to complete.

### Neural Training

Flow Nexus includes cloud-based neural training capabilities. You can train custom models, deploy them as templates, benchmark their performance, and run distributed training across clusters.

### Gamification

Flow Nexus includes a gamification layer with challenges, achievements, leaderboards, and a virtual currency (RUV). Complete challenges to earn RUV, which can be used within the platform.

## The 70+ MCP Tools

Flow Nexus exposes over 70 MCP (Model Context Protocol) tools that Claude Code and other MCP clients can call directly. These tools cover:

- Swarm management (init, list, status, scale, destroy)
- Neural operations (train, predict, deploy, benchmark)
- Sandbox operations (create, execute, configure, upload, logs)
- Workflow management (create, execute, status, audit)
- App store (list, publish, search, analytics)
- User management (register, login, profile, upgrade)
- Storage (upload, download, list, delete)

## How It Connects

- **Claude Flow**: The local orchestrator. Flow Nexus is the cloud extension -- same agents, same tools, but running remotely.
- **ruv-swarm**: Swarms can run in Flow Nexus's cloud infrastructure for distributed execution.
- **AgentDB**: Agent memory persists in the cloud, accessible across sessions and deployments.
- **AIMDS**: Security scanning runs on all cloud-deployed agents, protecting against attacks even in remote environments.
- **SONA**: Routing optimization works across cloud-deployed agents just as it does locally.

## Key Numbers

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| MCP tools | 70+ |
| Sandbox timeout | Up to 1 hour |
| Sandbox isolation | Full OS-level (E2B) |
| Workflow audit | Complete trail |
| Real-time streaming | Yes |
| App store | Publish, search, analytics |
| Neural training | Distributed clusters |
| Platform version | 0.1.128 |

## Why This Matters

Local development is great for building and testing. But production AI needs to run in the cloud -- reliably, securely, and at scale. Flow Nexus bridges the gap between "I built a cool agent on my laptop" and "my team uses AI agents in production every day." The sandboxed execution means you can trust it with production workloads, and the app store means you can share your best agents with others.

# What Is SONA? The Self-Tuning Brain That Optimizes Your AI Stack

## Why Should You Care?

Your AI system has access to multiple models (Haiku, Sonnet, Opus), multiple agent types (coder, tester, reviewer, architect), and multiple strategies for handling any given task. Without optimization, the system either always uses the most expensive option (wasteful) or always uses the cheapest option (poor quality). What you really want is a system that automatically picks the best option for each specific task -- and gets better at picking over time.

That is exactly what SONA does. SONA stands for Self-Optimizing Neural Architecture. It is the decision layer that watches every task, learns what works, and routes future tasks to the optimal combination of model, agent, and strategy -- all in under 0.05 milliseconds.

## The Thermostat Analogy

A thermostat does not just heat or cool. It SENSES the current temperature, COMPARES it to your desired temperature, and ADJUSTS accordingly. If you open a window, it adapts instantly. You never think about it, but it is constantly optimizing.

SONA does this for your entire AI stack:

- Task is simple? Route to Haiku (fast, cheap).
- Task is complex? Route to Opus (powerful, thorough).
- System under heavy load? Reduce parallelism to prevent bottlenecks.
- Pattern recognized from a previous task? Skip the research phase and apply the known solution directly.

All of this happens invisibly, in 0.05 milliseconds -- 50 microseconds. That is faster than the time it takes light to travel 15 kilometers. You never notice the optimization, but you benefit from it on every single request.

## How SONA Learns

SONA uses a technique called Mixture of Experts (MoE). Think of it as having 10 specialists in a room. When a new task arrives, SONA has learned which specialist handles which type of task best. It does not deliberate -- the right choice just "clicks" from experience, like an experienced manager who has worked with the same team for years.

The learning happens through three mechanisms:

### Two-Tier LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)

LoRA is a technique that fine-tunes a model by adjusting only a small number of parameters, rather than retraining the whole model. SONA uses two tiers: a fast tier that adapts in real time (under 1 millisecond) and a slow tier that consolidates learnings over longer periods. The fast tier handles immediate adjustments; the slow tier builds long-term expertise.

### EWC++ (Elastic Weight Consolidation)

One of the biggest problems in machine learning is "catastrophic forgetting" -- when learning new things causes the system to forget old things. EWC++ prevents this by identifying which learned parameters are most important and protecting them from being overwritten. SONA can learn new task routing patterns without losing what it already knows about old patterns.

### ReasoningBank Integration

Every routing decision SONA makes is recorded in the ReasoningBank. When a similar task appears later, SONA retrieves the past decision and its outcome. If the outcome was good, it reinforces the pattern. If it was bad, it adjusts. This creates a continuous improvement loop.

## Why 0.05ms Matters

If SONA took 1 second to decide how to route a task, you would notice the delay on every request. At 100 milliseconds, it would add up over a session with hundreds of tasks. At 0.05 milliseconds, the optimization is completely invisible. Every request benefits from intelligent routing with zero perceptible cost.

For perspective, a typical API call to Claude takes 500-5,000 milliseconds. SONA's routing decision is 10,000x to 100,000x faster than the actual work it is routing. The overhead is essentially zero.

## How It Connects

- **Claude Flow**: SONA runs inside Claude Flow, optimizing every task assignment in real time.
- **ReasoningBank**: Stores and retrieves SONA's routing decisions for continuous learning.
- **Agentic Flow**: The runtime that executes tasks using the model and agent type that SONA selected.
- **RuVector**: SONA uses RuVector embeddings to represent task characteristics in vector space for fast pattern matching.
- **AgentDB**: Agent performance data that SONA uses to evaluate which agents excel at which tasks.

## Key Numbers

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Routing decision time | 0.05ms (50 microseconds) |
| Real-time learning | Under 1ms |
| Learning technique | Two-Tier LoRA + EWC++ |
| Pattern matching | Mixture of Experts (MoE) |
| Overhead vs task execution | 10,000x - 100,000x faster |
| Forgetting prevention | EWC++ (Elastic Weight Consolidation) |
| Integration | ReasoningBank, RuVector, Claude Flow |

## Why This Matters

Without SONA, you would need to manually decide which model and agent type to use for each task -- or waste money by always using the most expensive option. SONA automates this decision with near-zero latency, saves 85-99% on API costs (via the Multi-Model Router it informs), and gets smarter every day. It is the reason the RuvNet ecosystem feels like it "just works" -- because behind every request, SONA is silently making the optimal choice.

# What Is ReasoningBank? How Your AI System Learns From Every Decision

## Why Should You Care?

Most AI systems treat every request as brand new. They do not remember that they solved a similar problem yesterday, or that a particular approach failed last week. They repeat the same mistakes and rediscover the same solutions, over and over.

ReasoningBank changes this. It is a pattern memory system that records every significant decision an AI agent makes -- what the situation was, what approach was chosen, whether it succeeded, and what was learned. The next time a similar situation appears, the agent retrieves those patterns and applies proven approaches instead of starting from scratch.

Think of it as the difference between a new employee and a ten-year veteran. Both are intelligent. But the veteran has a mental library of thousands of past situations and knows what works. ReasoningBank gives AI agents that mental library.

## The Recipe Book Analogy

Imagine a chef who writes down every dish they cook -- not just the recipe, but the conditions. "Used this technique when the kitchen was busy and had limited ingredients. It worked because..." Over years, the chef builds a recipe book indexed not just by dish name, but by situation. When faced with a new challenge, they flip through the book and find the recipe that matches their current constraints.

ReasoningBank is that recipe book for AI reasoning. Every pattern is indexed by meaning (using HNSW vector search), so agents find relevant past experience even when the current task uses completely different words.

## The Four-Step Pipeline

ReasoningBank operates through a continuous four-step pipeline called RETRIEVE-JUDGE-DISTILL-CONSOLIDATE:

### Step 1: RETRIEVE

When an agent faces a new task, ReasoningBank searches its pattern store for semantically similar past decisions. This is not keyword matching -- it uses HNSW-indexed vector search to find patterns that are similar in meaning. "Fix the authentication bug" matches patterns about "login failures," "session token issues," and "credential validation errors" because their vector representations are close together.

### Step 2: JUDGE

Retrieved patterns are evaluated for relevance and quality. Each pattern has a tracked success rate. A pattern that worked 95% of the time in similar situations gets high confidence. A pattern that worked only 60% of the time gets lower confidence. The agent uses the highest-confidence relevant patterns to inform its approach.

### Step 3: DISTILL

After the agent completes the task, the result is distilled into a new pattern. "When facing situation X, approach Y produced result Z with confidence W." This new pattern captures the essential learning without storing every detail of the interaction.

### Step 4: CONSOLIDATE

Over time, the pattern store grows. Consolidation merges similar patterns, prunes redundant ones, and strengthens high-value patterns. This prevents the store from growing indefinitely and ensures retrieval stays fast. It is like a librarian who periodically organizes the shelves, combining duplicate books and archiving rarely-read volumes.

## HNSW-Indexed Pattern Memory

The critical technology inside ReasoningBank is HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) indexing, powered by RuVector. Every pattern is converted into a vector embedding and indexed in a multi-layer graph structure. This means:

- **Search is fast**: Finding the most relevant patterns among millions takes sub-millisecond time.
- **Search is semantic**: Patterns match by meaning, not by keywords.
- **Search scales**: Whether you have 100 patterns or 10 million, retrieval speed remains consistent.

At 32.6 million operations per second, ReasoningBank can service every agent in a large swarm without becoming a bottleneck.

## The Self-Learning Loop

The four-step pipeline creates a self-learning loop. Every task produces new patterns (DISTILL). Every new task benefits from past patterns (RETRIEVE). Patterns that work get reinforced (JUDGE). The store stays organized (CONSOLIDATE). Over time, agents become demonstrably better:

- First attempt at a new task type: 70% success rate.
- After 5 learning iterations: 95% success rate.
- Execution speed improvement: 46% faster after learning.
- Learning velocity: 3.2x faster than systems without ReasoningBank.

This is not theoretical. These numbers come from measured performance across real agent workloads.

## How It Connects

- **RuVector**: Provides the HNSW engine that indexes all reasoning patterns for fast semantic retrieval.
- **AgentDB**: Stores the raw memories that ReasoningBank distills into patterns. AgentDB is the filing cabinet; ReasoningBank is the indexed summary.
- **SONA**: Uses ReasoningBank patterns to optimize routing decisions. If a pattern says "Opus handles this task type best," SONA routes accordingly.
- **AIMDS**: Uses the same RETRIEVE-JUDGE-DISTILL-CONSOLIDATE pipeline for security learning. Every attack pattern becomes a defensive pattern.
- **Agentic Flow**: Agents query ReasoningBank before every significant decision and store new patterns after completion.
- **Claude Flow**: The orchestrator that ensures ReasoningBank is consulted during task planning.

## Key Numbers

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Throughput | 32.6M operations/sec |
| Pipeline steps | 4 (Retrieve, Judge, Distill, Consolidate) |
| First-attempt success | 70% |
| After-learning success | 95% |
| Execution speedup | 46% faster |
| Learning velocity | 3.2x faster |
| Index type | HNSW (via RuVector) |
| Confidence decay | 1% per day (prevents stale patterns) |
| Pattern matching | Semantic (vector-based) |

## Why This Matters

ReasoningBank is what transforms AI agents from stateless tools into learning systems. Without it, every session starts from zero. With it, every session builds on everything that came before. The 46% execution speedup and the jump from 70% to 95% success rate are not marginal improvements -- they represent the difference between an AI that frustrates you with repeated mistakes and an AI that feels like a seasoned colleague who knows exactly what to do.
